Phase 1: Exploration Phase 2: Preparation Phase 3: Implementation Phase 4: Sustainment

Phase 1: Exploration

Identify human and environmental factors before implementing acupuncture or acupressure in a hospital setting.

Bottom Line Before launching any hospital acupuncture programme, you must understand the internal need, the available intervention options, your team composition, staff and patient attitudes, organisational readiness, and funding landscape. Skipping this phase is the most common reason implementations fail.

Overview

Before implementing acupuncture or acupressure in any hospital context, organisations must understand both the human and environmental factors at play. This phase addresses seven critical questions:

  • Does an internal need for change exist?
  • What intervention types are available?
  • Who should be on the implementation team?
  • What are staff perspectives on the proposed changes?
  • What are patient requirements and preferences?
  • Is funding necessary and available?
  • Is the organisation ready for change?

1. Identify Internal Needs

Assessing current practice (using PONV as an example)

Using PONV (post-operative nausea and vomiting) as a worked example, the following questions help establish baseline needs. Adapt these to your target clinical application (e.g., pain management, anxiety, post-procedural recovery).

How is the condition currently prevented and managed locally?

  • Review hospital practice guidelines for the target condition
  • Identify current pharmacological and non-pharmacological approaches

Is the condition incidence charted?

  • Check observation charts across all relevant care settings (e.g., Recovery, ward, pre-discharge)
  • Verify documentation is consistent across phases of care

What is the current incidence and rescue medication usage?

  • Track current incident rates
  • Monitor rescue medication administration
  • Note: rescue medication use is not equivalent to the condition incidence itself
PONV risk assessment (example)

For PONV specifically, risk assessment is the first recommendation in management guidelines (Gan, 2014). Four primary risk factors have been identified:

  • Female gender
  • Non-smoker status
  • Previous PONV or motion sickness history
  • Post-operative opioid use

Risk increases approximately 20% per additional factor present (Apfel et al., 1999). A patient self-assessment checklist can streamline this process.

2. Intervention Types: Acupuncture and Acupressure Modalities

Several modalities are available for hospital acupuncture implementation. The table below compares key options, with PONV effectiveness data as an illustrative example of the evidence base. Similar evidence profiles exist for pain, anxiety, and other conditions.

Manual Acupressure

Advantages: Avoids needles; requires no equipment; staff are easily trained.

Disadvantages: Effectiveness varies by practitioner skill and consistency.

Electroacupressure (E-Stim)

PONV effectiveness example: Nausea reduced from 45% to 30% (RR 0.71, 95% CI 0.62–0.81) High; Vomiting reduced from 30% to 18% (RR 0.60, 95% CI 0.50–0.73) High

Advantages: Extended-duration capability; adjustable intensity.

Disadvantages: Equipment cost ($40–$200 AUD); training required.

Auricular Acupuncture (Ear)

Advantages: Economical (a few cents per needle); individualised point selection; low needle misplacement risk.

Disadvantages: Staff training required; a qualified acupuncturist may be needed.

Shown effective for gynaecological surgery and laparoscopic cholecystectomy (PONV context).

Body Acupuncture (PC6 + Other Points)

PONV effectiveness example: Nausea reduced from 54% to 29% (RR 0.56, 95% CI 0.39–0.80) Moderate; Vomiting reduced from 41% to 20% (RR 0.51, 95% CI 0.34–0.76) Moderate

Combined therapy (PC6 + additional acupoints) reduced PONV from 21% to 6% (RR 0.29, 95% CI 0.17–0.49) Moderate

Advantages: Economical (~$0.05 per needle); individualised point selection.

Disadvantages: Training requirement; needle monitoring needed during treatment.

Electroacupuncture (EA)

Effectiveness is equivalent to body acupuncture, with the benefit of adjustable stimulus intensity.

Advantages: Reusable equipment; adjustable intensity.

Disadvantages: Higher equipment cost ($200–$800 AUD); monitoring required.

Acupressure Wristbands

PONV effectiveness example: Nausea reduced from 36% to 24% (RR 0.60, 95% CI 0.53–0.69) High; Vomiting reduced from 20% to 15% (RR 0.54, 95% CI 0.45–0.64) High; Rescue antiemetics reduced from 22% to 13% (RR 0.62, 95% CI 0.52–0.74) Moderate

Advantages: Cost-effective (~$5 AUD); extended-use capability; no infection risk; minimal training; patients can continue use at home.

Disadvantages: Limited sizing for large wrists; impractical with multiple IV lines; potential wrist injuries.

Comparative Summary: PC6 Stimulation (All Modalities Combined)
Outcome Control Intervention Effect (RR, 95% CI) Evidence
Nausea 47% 31% 0.68 (0.60–0.77) High
Vomiting 33% 19% 0.60 (0.51–0.71) High
Rescue antiemetics 33% 20% 0.64 (0.55–0.73) Moderate

PONV data shown as a worked example. Source: Lee et al., 2015 (Cochrane). Side effects are mild and transient. Efficacy is equivalent to common antiemetics and enhanced when combined with pharmacological approaches.

Cost Comparison of Modalities
Modality Approximate Cost (AUD) Reusable?
Acupuncture needles~$0.05 per needleNo
Acupressure wristbands~$5 per unitYes
Electroacupressure units$40–$200Yes
Electroacupuncture units$200–$800Yes
Auricular needlesA few cents per needleNo

3. The Implementation Team

Key Team Roles

Successful implementation requires individuals fulfilling three critical roles:

Opinion Leaders / Facilitators

Individuals within the organisation who have formal or informal influence on attitudes and beliefs regarding intervention implementation.

Champions

Individuals who drive through an implementation, overcoming indifference or resistance from colleagues and institutional inertia.

External Change Agents

External individuals who formally influence or facilitate intervention decisions — for example, an acupuncture researcher supporting a hospital team.

Key principle: Early adopters should share professional, educational, cultural, or socioeconomic backgrounds with leadership for successful uptake.

4. Staff Assessment

Staff Groups Requiring Involvement
  • Anaesthetists
  • Nurses
  • Midwives
  • Gynaecologists / Obstetricians
  • Surgeons
  • Administrative clerks
  • Interpreters

The specific groups will vary depending on the clinical application. For a pain management programme, physiotherapists and pain specialists would also be relevant.

Survey Evidence: Staff Attitudes (USA)

A survey of 292 anaesthesia staff across 96 departments in the USA (Faircloth, 2014) found broadly positive attitudes toward hospital acupuncture:

FindingResult
Respondent composition54% anaesthesiologists, 44% nurses, 2% aides
Prior use with positive outcomes27%
Willing to consider use54%
Willing to pursue education74%

Main Barriers Identified

Barrier% Reporting
Lack of scientific evidence (perceived)79%
Unavailability of credentialled providers71%
Lack of equipment49%
Too time-consuming38%
Survey Evidence: Staff Attitudes (Australia)

An Australian hospital survey of 165 doctors, nurses, midwives, and obstetricians found:

FindingResult
Believed acupuncture effective for PONV42%
Would encourage patients after education81%
Wanted further education88%

In the broader Australian primary care context, ~85% of 544 surveyed GPs considered acupuncture effective and safe (Cohen et al., 2005), and 80% have referred patients for acupuncture (Wardle et al., 2013). Some Australian medical schools already teach acupuncture as electives.

5. Organisational Readiness Assessment

Using the CARI Checklist

The Modified Checklist to Assess Organisation Readiness (CARI) (Barwick, 2011) evaluates five domains:

  • System readiness
  • Senior leadership support
  • Staff capability
  • Implementation plan maturity
  • Training requirements

Scoring: Total possible = 100 points. Readiness threshold is 80+ points, with a minimum of 15 points per category. Scores below 80 indicate areas that need strengthening before proceeding to Phase 2.

6. Cost and Funding Considerations

Cost Assessment Framework

Based on Myle (2016), the following costs should be tracked for any hospital acupuncture programme:

Cost CategoryWhat to Track
Device/consumable costCost per patient per session
Staff time — deliveryMinutes per intervention delivery
Staff time — monitoringMinutes per monitoring period
Existing medicationsPreventive and rescue medication types, dosages, and costs
EquipmentCapital cost of reusable devices

Compare the total cost of acupuncture/acupressure delivery against the existing pharmacological pathway to assess whether the intervention is cost-neutral or cost-saving.

7. Patient Perspectives

Patient Survey Evidence

A survey of 160 Australian surgical patients (Weeks et al., 2017) found strong patient receptivity to hospital acupuncture, particularly after brief education:

MeasureResult
Pre-education willingness to use acupuncture65%
Baseline knowledge that acupuncture can treat nausea/vomiting15%
Post-education willingness87% (+22 percentage points)

Clinical guidelines recommend considering patient preferences when selecting treatment approaches. Brief education can substantially increase patient acceptance.

Phase 1 Resources

  • PONV risk self-assessment checklist
  • Data extraction template (Excel)
  • Modified CARI readiness checklist
  • Sample staff survey questions
  • Patient survey results and template
